title: Working with your vet: flock health planning
subtitle: What a flock health plan is and how to build one with your vet.
excerpt: A flock health plan is a written document agreed between a livestock keeper and their vet that sets out the vaccination, worming, and monitoring schedule for the holding. It's a legal requirement for some farm assurance schemes and good practice for any livestock keeper. Setting one up for the first time takes one vet visit.
sourceNotes: APHA Vet Gateway flock health planning resources, gov.uk. RCVS Practice Standards for farm veterinary practice. AHDB Better Returns Programme veterinary partnership guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A written health management document for a livestock holding, agreed between keeper and vet, covering disease prevention, treatment protocols, and monitoring schedules.
Medicines, including vaccines and parasiticides, that can only be obtained and used under veterinary prescription. A vet-keeper relationship and health plan is a prerequisite for prescription access.

content.text: A
flock health plan
is not a paperwork exercise. It is the agreement between you and your vet about what you are going to do to keep your animals healthy, and what the vet will do when they cannot.
Why it matters
UK rules from November 2022 require all keepers of food-producing animals to have a written health plan. Without one, you cannot get prescription-only
veterinary medicines
, including worming treatments. If you sell meat, milk, or eggs, or belong to a farm assurance scheme, this applies to you.

For non-commercial small holdings it is still good practice. A relationship with a farm vet who knows your holding means faster support in an emergency and better-targeted advice on disease prevention.
What a plan covers
Species and numbers on the holding.
Vaccination schedule, which vaccines, which species, which months.
Worming programme, what product class, frequency, and whether a resistance testing protocol is in place.
Ectoparasite (lice, mite, fly) control, products and timings.
Key disease risks for your location and species, your vet knows what is circulating locally.
Emergency contact protocols, out-of-hours cover, notifiable disease procedures.
Setting up the first visit

Call a farm practice (not a small-animal practice) and book a farm visit. Bring a list of all species and numbers, any health records you already have, and a list of any medicines you are currently using. The vet will walk the holding, assess your facilities, and co-author the plan with you on the visit.

The annual farm visit for the health plan is not the same as an emergency call-out. Many practices offer a discounted fixed fee for the annual planning visit. Over the course of the year, this is good value compared with the cost of treating preventable disease.
Reviewing and updating

The plan should be reviewed annually, or whenever the species or numbers on the holding change significantly. A holding that adds pigs for the first time or introduces a dairy goat needs the plan updated to cover the new species. The vet countersigns any prescription changes.
